blocks.content: Davenport interviewed them one by one. An hour and forty minutes in total, working through each account the way he worked through everything — methodically, without showing his hand.

Cade Mercer: grief raw and close to the surface. He'd been the one to knock, the one to push, the one to say break it — and he was carrying that. His timeline was consistent, his distress unmanaged, the kind that doesn't perform. When he talked about Marcus on the beach that afternoon his voice went somewhere else entirely. Last person to see him alive. First one to know something was wrong. Davenport noted both.

Dean Ashby: controlled, factual, gave his account in sequence without being prompted to. The kind of witness who tells you exactly what happened and nothing more — not because he's hiding anything, but because that's how his mind works. He'd broken the door. His shoulder would be bruised tomorrow and he hadn't mentioned it once.

Sophie Laurent: emotional in the way that makes everything harder to read. She'd gone upstairs alone to shower during the afternoon — forty minutes, she thought, maybe more. She wasn't certain of the time. She offered the uncertainty without being asked, which was either honest or careful. Davenport wrote down both possibilities.

Wren Cole: few words, precise ones. She had been watching Marcus on the beach. She described him as looking like someone hearing bad news in an empty room. She didn't elaborate on that. She answered exactly what she was asked, and when he was done asking she folded her hands in her lap and waited.

Nadia Reyes: composed. She'd had a phone call with Marcus a few weeks back — he'd mentioned trouble sleeping. She offered it plainly, without drama. Everything she said checked out against the others' accounts. Nothing contradicted. Nothing inflated.
Five people. Five versions of the same evening, each one slightly different in the details that didn't matter and consistent where it counted. No obvious gaps. No obvious lies.
Which, in Davenport's experience, meant the gaps were somewhere he hadn't looked yet.
